description: This figure presents engineering stress–strain curves for Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels with varying nitrogen contents, specifically comparing low-nitrogen (LN) and high-nitrogen (HN) compositions. The graph displays six tensile curves: LN1, LN2, and LN3 (represented by dashed lines in green, gray, and yellow, respectively) and HN1, HN2, and HN3 (represented by solid lines in black, red, and blue, respectively). All specimens exhibit continuous yielding and pronounced strain hardening behavior typical of austenitic steels. The LN steels demonstrate higher yield strength (~685 MPa) but fracture at lower strains (~60–65%), while the HN steels show slightly lower yield strength (~647 MPa) but significantly enhanced ductility with fracture strains reaching ~75–80%. The ultimate tensile strengths are comparable, with LN steels at ~832.5 MPa and HN steels at ~841.5 MPa. The HN curves maintain higher stress levels over extended strain ranges and display larger necking regions, indicating superior uniform elongation and total elongation compared to LN steels. These results support the conclusion that nitrogen addition refines austenite grains and promotes deformation twinning, thereby enhancing plasticity, fracture toughness, and impact energy (130.5 J vs. 81 J) despite reducing yield strength due to decreased ferrite content from 5.08% to 1.84%.
